UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user account within the TezTerminal Antigravity application, storing personal settings and preferences.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

username.description: A unique username chosen by the user.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the user profile was created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the user profile was last updated.

preferredCurrency.description: The user's preferred currency for financial displays (e.g., USD, EUR).

timezone.description: The user's preferred timezone for displaying timestamps (e.g., America/New_York).

WebhookConfiguration.title: WebhookConfiguration
WebhookConfiguration.description: Defines a specific webhook endpoint created by a user to receive trading signals from external services like TradingView.

id.description: Unique identifier for the WebhookConfiguration entity.

name.description: A user-friendly name for this webhook configuration, facilitating identification.

endpointUrl.description: The unique URL where external services (e.g., TradingView) should send alerts. This URL is generated by the system for security and uniqueness.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile who owns and manages this webhook configuration. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N WebhookConfiguration)

description.description: An optional, user-provided description for this webhook configuration, detailing its purpose or associated alerts.

createdAt.description: Timestamp when the webhook configuration was initially created.

updatedAt.description: Timestamp when the webhook configuration was last updated.

isActive.description: Indicates whether the webhook is currently active and capable of receiving events (true) or disabled (false).

secretKey.description: A secret key or token that external services should include in their requests to validate incoming webhook requests, ensuring they come from an authorized source.

expectedSource.description: An optional field to specify the expected source of the webhook (e.g., 'TradingView') for additional validation, categorization, or specific processing logic.

WebhookEvent.title: WebhookEvent
WebhookEvent.description: Records each incoming trading signal or alert received via a configured webhook, preserving the raw data and processing status.

id.description: Unique identifier for the WebhookEvent entity.

webhookConfigurationId.description: Reference to the WebhookConfiguration that successfully received this event. (Relationship: WebhookConfiguration 1:N WebhookEvent)

receivedAt.description: Timestamp when the webhook event was received by the TezTerminal Antigravity application.

payload.description: The full raw payload (e.g., JSON string) received from the webhook. This contains the actual trading signal or alert data.

sourceIp.description: The IP address from which the webhook request originated, useful for security audits and debugging.

headers.description: A stringified JSON object containing relevant HTTP headers from the incoming webhook request, providing context about the request.

processingStatus.description: The current processing status of the webhook event (e.g., 'PENDING', 'PROCESSED', 'FAILED', 'IGNORED').

errorMessage.description: An optional field to store error messages if the processing of the webhook event failed, aiding in troubleshooting.

definition.description: Stores individual user profile settings and preferences. This is the root document for a user's data. Access is strictly restricted to the owning user, where the document ID `{userId}` matches `request.auth.uid`.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user, matching `request.auth.uid`.

definition.description: User-specific webhook configurations created by the user to receive trading signals. Includes denormalized 'userId' (matching path and `request.auth.uid`) for authorization independence. Only the owning user can create, read, update, or delete their configurations. The 'endpointUrl' and 'secretKey' fields must be protected from unauthorized reads.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the owning user, matching `request.auth.uid`.

params.name: webhookConfigurationId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific webhook configuration.

definition.description: Records individual incoming webhook events (trading signals). Includes denormalized 'userId' (matching path and `request.auth.uid`) for authorization independence, allowing the owning user to list and view their events without needing to query the parent webhook configuration. The 'webhookConfigurationId' is retained for data integrity and specific queries within the user's events (e.g., all events for a specific configuration). The 'payload' and 'headers' fields must be protected for privacy.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the owning user, matching `request.auth.uid`.

params.name: webhookEventId
params.description: The unique identifier for a specific incoming webhook event.

firestore.reasoning:
The recommended Firestore structure prioritizes robust security rules, scalability, and debuggability by strictly adhering to the core design principles. All user-specific data is encapsulated under a top-level `/users/{userId}` collection, which serves as the foundational unit of authorization.

**Authorization Independence (CRITICAL):**
1.  **UserProfile:** Each user's profile is stored as a single document at `/users/{userId}`, where `{userId}` directly corresponds to `request.auth.uid`. This makes authorization trivial: `allow read, update, delete: if request.auth.uid == userId; allow create: if request.auth.uid != null;` (assuming the document ID matches `userId`). This eliminates any need for `get()` calls in rules to verify ownership.
2.  **WebhookConfiguration:** These are stored in a subcollection at `/users/{userId}/webhookConfigurations/{webhookConfigurationId}`. The `WebhookConfiguration` entity schema already includes a `userId` field. This `userId` will be denormalized into *each* `webhookConfiguration` document and, critically, matches the `{userId}` wildcard in the path. This allows rules to authorize access (create, read, update, delete) to `webhookConfigurations` by simply comparing `request.auth.uid` with `userId` from the path or the document itself, without needing `get()` calls on the parent user profile.
3.  **WebhookEvent:** Similar to `WebhookConfiguration`, these are stored in a subcollection at `/users/{userId}/webhookEvents/{webhookEventId}`. The `WebhookEvent` entity schema includes `webhookConfigurationId`. To achieve authorization independence at the `WebhookEvent` level, the `userId` (from the owning `UserProfile` and `WebhookConfiguration`) MUST be denormalized into *each* `webhookEvent` document. This `userId` field in the document will match the `{userId}` wildcard in the path. This allows rules to authorize access to `webhookEvents` by comparing `request.auth.uid` with the `userId` in the path or the document, again eliminating `get()` calls.

**Query As Privilege (QAPs) Support:**
This structure inherently supports QAPs for `list` operations:
1.  **UserProfile:** A user can only access their own profile at `/users/$(request.auth.uid)`. Listing is not applicable here as it's a single document.
2.  **WebhookConfiguration:** A user can `list` all their webhook configurations by querying `/users/$(request.auth.uid)/webhookConfigurations`. Because the rules verify `request.auth.uid == userId` (from the path), Firestore will only return configurations owned by the authenticated user, without requiring any client-side filtering or complex server-side logic in rules.
3.  **WebhookEvent:** Similarly, a user can `list` all their webhook events by querying `/users/$(request.auth.uid)/webhookEvents`. The denormalized `userId` in each `WebhookEvent` document, matching the path's `userId`, allows rules to enforce that only events belonging to `request.auth.uid` are returned, satisfying QAPs for event history display.

**Structural Segregation:** Each collection (`users`, `webhookConfigurations`, `webhookEvents`) has a homogeneous security posture (owner-only access), simplifying rules significantly and avoiding mixed access patterns within a single collection.
